claims.claim: The Kampung Baru master redevelopment plan remains stalled.
claims.source: PKB repost of Bernama/Sinar Harian, 'New development approach for Kampung Baru', updated 14 May 2026, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374; Bernama, 21 Aug 2020, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=1872479
claims.correction: The analysis should specify that the 2019 concept-land purchase approach stalled, and that PKB/government shifted to PIPKB 2040 and phased priority-area development rather than no redevelopment activity at all.
claims.stale_check: As of PKB page updated 14 May 2026, the 2019 concept plan was still described as difficult to pursue, but PKB also lists current, completed, construction, and approval-stage projects in Kampong Bharu.

claims.claim: The Kampung Baru master redevelopment plan is a 121-hectare project.
claims.source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2476514
claims.correction: PKB states its Kampong Bharu operational area is 300.73 acres, about 121.7 ha, but the Malay Agricultural Settlement area is 219.91 acres, about 89 ha. The card should distinguish PKB's operational area from MAS/Kampung Baru proper.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found; the distinction remains important in 2025-2026 official statements.

claims.claim: The Kampung Baru master redevelopment plan is costed at around RM43 billion.
claims.source: No primary source found within reasonable effort. Secondary references mention RM43 billion, but PKB/Bernama sources reviewed instead cite RM7.4 billion land purchase and nearly RM10 billion total costs for the 2019 purchase approach: https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374
claims.correction: Either cite the original PITPKB/PIPKB document page that states RM43 billion, or replace with the verified 2019 figures: estimated RM7.4 billion land purchase cost and nearly RM10 billion including other costs.
claims.stale_check: The RM43 billion figure appears tied to older master-plan reporting; current official materials reviewed emphasize PIPKB 2040, phased development, and land acquisition cost estimates.

claims.claim: The master plan envisages around RM43 billion across 121 hectares with 17,500 residential units.
claims.source: No primary source found within reasonable effort. Secondary sources mention 17,500 units and RM43 billion, but the accessible PKB sources did not verify both figures together.
claims.correction: Provide a direct official PKB/PITPKB page or PDF citation for RM43 billion and 17,500 units. Without that, mark this as an older reported plan rather than a verified current official plan.
claims.stale_check: Possible stale-plan issue: Bernama in 2019 reported a different concept involving 45,000 houses, and PKB/Bernama in 2020 said the 2019 concept was difficult to pursue.

claims.claim: PKB's separate Kampung Sungai Baru sub-project involved 328 owners.
claims.source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2476514
claims.correction: Use '328 property owners/units comprising 64 terrace-lot owners and 264 flat-unit owners' for precision.
claims.stale_check: No material recent change found to the 328 figure.

claims.claim: PKB's separate Kampung Sungai Baru sub-project finally moved forward in 2025 after years of dispute.
claims.source: Bernama, 30 Nov 2025, https://bernama.com/bm/am/news.php?id=2497197; Bernama, 12 Sep 2025, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php/general/news.php?id=2466997; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=2476605
claims.correction: Specify the project officially began with a groundbreaking ceremony on 30 November 2025 and was expected to complete within four years.
claims.stale_check: This is a post-analysis relevant update if the card was drafted before 30 November 2025. As of 17 May 2026, the claim that it moved forward in 2025 is accurate.

claims.claim: The larger Kampung Baru proper has not begun.
claims.source: PKB Current Development Project page, last updated 11 May 2026, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en
claims.correction: If the intended claim is that the full master redevelopment has not begun, say that. PKB lists Kampong Bharu-area projects as completed, under construction, and at approval/amendment stage, so 'has not begun' is too broad.
claims.stale_check: Current PKB page as of 11 May 2026 contradicts a blanket 'not begun' framing.

claims.claim: 40 years, zero ground broken on main Kampung Baru.
claims.source: Bernama, 1 Mar 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2059019; PKB Current Development Project page, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en
claims.correction: Earlier redevelopment efforts were first mooted in 1984, but 'zero ground broken' overstates the case because PKB lists completed, construction-stage and plan-approval projects in Kampong Bharu. Use 'the full master redevelopment has not been delivered' instead.
claims.stale_check: Not a stale issue; the latest PKB project list makes the absolute wording misleading.

claims.claim: Fragmented Malay Reserve Land ownership slows consolidation.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2476514; PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950
claims.correction: Kampung Baru proper is described officially as Malay Agricultural Settlement land with Malay ownership restrictions, not simply Malay Reserve Land. Kampung Sungai Baru is explicitly not MAS or Malay reserve land.
claims.stale_check: Recent 2025 official statements clarify the land-status distinction, so older shorthand as 'Malay reserve land' should be corrected.

claims.claim: 121 hectares of Kampung Baru sit beside the KLCC core.
claims.source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745
claims.correction: Use 'PKB's 300.73-acre operational area, about 121.7 ha' rather than implying the MAS core alone is 121 ha.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found.

claims.claim: Kampung Baru is legally complicated by Malay Reserve Land status.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745; PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2476514
claims.correction: Replace 'Malay Reserve Land status' with 'Malay Agricultural Settlement status and Malay-only ownership restrictions'. Also state Kampung Sungai Baru is not MAS or Malay reserve land.
claims.stale_check: Official clarification in October 2025 makes this correction especially important.

claims.claim: Land in Kampung Baru is some of the most valuable in KL on a per-square-foot basis.
claims.source: PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950; Bernama, 21 Sep 2019, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=1769924
claims.correction: PKB says valuation is constrained by Malay-only ownership restrictions, multiple ownership, uneconomic lot sizes, infrastructure and limited market. Bernama reported RM850 per sq ft as the highest JPPH valuation given those restrictions, not that it is among KL's highest psf land values overall.
claims.stale_check: No current official valuation ranking found. Treat as unsupported unless JPPH/NAPIC data is cited.

claims.claim: The master plan has not produced a single completed redevelopment block.
claims.source: PKB Current Development Project page, last updated 11 May 2026, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en
claims.correction: PKB lists completed projects in Kampong Bharu, including a 10-storey commercial building and MRT-related project, plus construction-stage projects. If the claim means 'no completed block under the full master-plan land-purchase scheme', it should say so and cite PKB.
claims.stale_check: Current PKB project list weakens the absolute claim.

claims.claim: Thousands of Malay families have lived in Kampung Baru for generations.
claims.source: Bernama, 7 Nov 2019, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=1787155; Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745
claims.correction: Officially verified figures found include PKB's 2010 census of 18,372 residents and the 1899 founding. I did not find a primary source verifying 'thousands of families' specifically.
claims.stale_check: No recent population/family count from a primary source was found.

claims.claim: Any redevelopment means displacing a historic community.
claims.source: PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950; Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745
claims.correction: Redevelopment can involve relocation and disruption, but PKB describes phased development, Malay ownership safeguards, and heritage components. 'Any redevelopment means displacing' is too categorical.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue; current plans emphasize phased and heritage-preserving approaches.

claims.claim: Cultural heritage value is real and is the central reason consolidation has been politically difficult across multiple administrations.
claims.source: Bernama, 13 Mar 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2278745; Bernama, 1 Mar 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2059019
claims.correction: Heritage is officially recognised as a planning concern, but Bernama/PKB also identify land restrictions, small lots, ancestral property issues, multiple ownership and consent problems as obstacles. Calling heritage the central reason is unsupported.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found.

claims.claim: PKB (Perbadanan Pembangunan Kampong Bharu) was set up in 2012.
claims.source: PKB Vision, Mission & Objectives page, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=301&lang=en; Act 733 page/PDF via PKB, https://www.pkb.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/Akta-733-BM.pdf
claims.correction: For precision: Act 733 was passed by Parliament on 7 December 2011 and PKB was established on 1 April 2012.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found.

claims.claim: PKB has not delivered the master redevelopment despite years of operational spending.
claims.source: PKB Current Development Project page, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en; PKB Vision, Mission & Objectives page, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=301&lang=en
claims.correction: It is fair to say the full master redevelopment has not been delivered, but 'despite years of operational spending' needs audited expenditure figures and context. PKB also lists completed and ongoing projects.
claims.stale_check: No audited spending source was cited in the analysis or found in accessible primary excerpts.

claims.claim: Malay Reserve Land status creates legal complexity requiring individual consent from a large number of fragmented lot owners.
claims.source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; PKB/Bernama new approach, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374; PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950
claims.correction: The legal issue is not simply Malay Reserve Land. PKB says no percentage of landowner consent is required for acquisition under the Land Acquisition Act 1960, though the ministry imposed a 50 percent consent threshold for Kampong Bharu redevelopment projects, and the 2019 purchase approach was difficult because it lacked 100 percent owner consent.
claims.stale_check: Recent official explanations continue to distinguish consent for acquisition from policy thresholds and negotiated purchase approaches.

claims.claim: Fragmented ownership and competing visions have paralysed redevelopment for four decades.
claims.source: Bernama, 1 Mar 2022, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2059019; PKB/Bernama, 21 Aug 2020, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374
claims.correction: Use less absolute language: official sources say earlier efforts hit a snag since first mooted in 1984 and cite land restrictions, small lots, ancestral property, multiple ownership and consent issues.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found for the historical obstacle claim, but current phased projects mean 'paralysed' should be limited to the full master redevelopment.

claims.claim: The Kg Sungai Baru breakthrough shows it can move when terms are right.
claims.source: Bernama, 30 Nov 2025, https://bernama.com/bm/am/news.php?id=2497197; PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720
claims.correction: Kampung Sungai Baru did move, but partly through land acquisition, compensation, evictions, court/legal processes and government intervention, not merely because 'terms are right'.
claims.stale_check: The November 2025 groundbreaking confirms movement, but earlier and continuing disputes should be acknowledged.

claims.claim: The path forward is structured smaller-block consolidation like Kg Sungai Baru, with cultural heritage protections built into each phase.
claims.source: PKB/Bernama new approach, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374; PKB FAQ, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=950
claims.correction: This is a policy judgment. It aligns partly with official phased priority-area language and heritage components, but the claim that Kg Sungai Baru is the model path forward is not verified as an official conclusion.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: a 121-hectare project costed at around RM43 billion
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The 121 ha figure is close to PKB's 300.73-acre operational area, but RM43 billion was not verified from an accessible primary source; current official sources cite different land-purchase cost figures.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; PKB/Bernama new approach, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 40 years, zero ground broken on main Kampung Baru
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Earlier plans have stalled since 1984, but PKB lists completed, construction-stage and approval-stage projects in the Kampong Bharu area, making 'zero ground broken' too absolute.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: PKB Current Development Project page, last updated 11 May 2026, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: legally complicated by Malay Reserve Land status
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Official sources describe Kampung Baru proper as Malay Agricultural Settlement with Malay ownership restrictions, while Kampung Sungai Baru is explicitly not MAS or Malay reserve land. The card conflates legal categories.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: the master plan has not produced a single completed redevelopment block
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This may be intended to refer narrowly to the full master-plan scheme, but as written it ignores PKB-listed completed Kampong Bharu projects.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Thousands of Malay families have lived in Kampung Baru for generations
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The historical Malay settlement claim is supported, and a 2010 resident count of 18,372 is reported by Bernama, but 'thousands of families' was not verified from a primary source.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The Kg Sungai Baru breakthrough shows it can move when terms are right.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The wording omits that the project proceeded amid land acquisition, compensation disputes, evictions and legal processes, not simply voluntary agreement on acceptable terms.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720; Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://www.bernama.com/en/general/news.php?id=2476605

omitted_facts: Kampung Sungai Baru is not part of Malay Agricultural Settlement land and has never been gazetted as Malay reserve land; it emerged after a 1967 Klang River straightening project and lies outside the 219.91-acre MAS area. Source: Bernama, 9 Oct 2025, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2476514

PKB says Kampung Sungai Baru covers 12.93 acres within PKB's 300.73-acre operational area and is outside MAS, with 99-year leasehold tenure. Source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720

The Kampung Sungai Baru redevelopment project officially began with a groundbreaking ceremony on 30 Nov 2025 and was expected to complete within four years. Source: Bernama, 30 Nov 2025, https://bernama.com/bm/am/news.php?id=2497197

PKB lists current, completed, construction-stage and plan-approval/amendment-stage projects in Kampong Bharu, including Sungai Baru as a new development project and other completed/construction-stage projects. Source: PKB Current Development Project page, last updated 11 May 2026, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?page_id=775&lang=en

The 2019 RM1,000 per sq ft land-purchase approach was difficult to pursue because it lacked 100 percent owner consent and received 61 percent agreement; estimated land-purchase cost was RM7.4 billion and total costs nearly RM10 billion. Source: PKB/Bernama, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=1374

For Kampong Bharu redevelopment projects, PKB says the ministry set a policy threshold of at least 50 percent owner consent, while the Land Acquisition Act 1960 itself does not require a percentage of owner consent before land acquisition. Source: PKB media statement, 15 Jun 2022, https://www.pkb.gov.my/?p=4720
source_assessment: The original analysis cites zero sources, so source diversity is effectively absent. The fact-check had to reconstruct support from PKB, Bernama reports of government/parliamentary statements, PKB FAQs and current project lists. These are mostly government or state news sources; they are close to primary for official positions but do not represent dissenting residents, opposition politicians, independent land valuers, legal scholars, developers, or heritage experts. Major gaps are the original PITPKB/PIPKB master-plan PDF evidence for RM43 billion and 17,500 units, audited PKB expenditure figures, and direct land valuation data.
overall_assessment: The analysis captures the broad truth that full Kampung Baru master redevelopment has repeatedly stalled and that Kampung Sungai Baru moved in 2025, but it contains several publication-risk errors and overclaims. The most serious are conflating Malay Agricultural Settlement with Malay Reserve Land, using unverified RM43 billion and 17,500-unit figures without a primary source, saying 'zero ground broken' despite PKB-listed projects, and framing Sungai Baru as a clean breakthrough without the land acquisition and dispute context. It is directionally plausible but not reliable enough for publication without corrections and sourcing.
